name: Dental Office Cleaning
criticalZone: operatory surfaces, suction lines, sterilisation area clean/dirty divide
regulatoryFramework: CDC Guidelines for Infection Control in Dental Health-Care Settings (2003) and the 2016 Summary
234 CMR — Massachusetts Board of Registration in Dentistry
accreditationBodies: Massachusetts Board of Registration in Dentistry

commonSurveyFindings.finding: No written policies and procedures for routine cleaning and disinfection of environmental surfaces as part of the practice's infection prevention plan.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016), Environmental Infection Prevention and Control

commonSurveyFindings.finding: Surface barriers on hard-to-clean clinical contact surfaces — chair switches, light handles, computer equipment — not changed between patients, or the surface underneath never checked for soiling once the barrier comes off.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016), Environmental Infection Prevention and Control

commonSurveyFindings.finding: Non-barrier-protected clinical contact surfaces not cleaned and disinfected with an EPA-registered hospital disinfectant after each patient.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016)

commonSurveyFindings.finding: A low-level disinfectant used where blood contamination is visible. Blood-contaminated clinical contact surfaces require an EPA-registered intermediate-level disinfectant with a tuberculocidal claim.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: CDC Best Practices for Environmental Infection Prevention and Control, Dental — clinical contact surfaces

commonSurveyFindings.finding: Manufacturer instructions for the disinfectant not followed — wrong dilution, wrong contact time, expired or badly stored solution, or solutions not made up fresh daily where the label requires it.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016) — amount, dilution, contact time, safe use and disposal

commonSurveyFindings.finding: Regulated medical waste not segregated or contained correctly — blood-soaked gauze and extracted teeth in ordinary trash rather than a leak-resistant biohazard bag, sharps outside a labelled puncture-resistant container.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: CDC Best Practices, disposing of medical waste; OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(4)(iii)

commonSurveyFindings.finding: Dental treatment water not maintained to drinking-water quality of 500 CFU/mL or fewer, or waterline treatment and monitoring not performed to the manufacturer's schedule. Independent water bottles alone are not sufficient.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: CDC Summary of Infection Prevention Practices in Dental Settings (2016), Dental Unit Water Quality

commonSurveyFindings.finding: No written schedule for cleaning and method of decontamination determined and implemented for the practice.
commonSurveyFindings.citation: 29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(4)(i); Massachusetts adopts this at 234 CMR 5.05(2), and 234 CMR 5.05(1) requires compliance with the CDC dental infection control guidelines
commonSurveyFindings.frequency: Federal OSHA cited 29 CFR 1910.1030 twelve times across seven inspections of dental offices between October 2024 and September 2025 — the most-cited standard for the sector.

_publishGate: Requires >=10 faqs and >=6 commonSurveyFindings before this service may go live. Survey findings are real regulatory citations — never invented, never inferred from the regulation text. Source: data/_todo-clinical-input.md.
